caption.text:
8,586 meters. The third-highest mountain on Earth. And on the Indian side, nobody is allowed to climb it.

Kangchenjunga has been worshipped by the Lepcha people of Sikkim for centuries. They call it Sewalungma — the mountain from which greetings are conveyed. They believe it is the birthplace of humanity, the abode of Dzoe-nga, the patron deity of Sikkim. Buddhists say the saint Padmasambhava recognized it as a protective spirit. To climb it is not achievement. It is trespass.

The first ascent in 1955 was made by Joe Brown and George Band — but they stopped just short of the true summit, keeping a promise to the Chogyal of Sikkim that the top would remain inviolate. In 2000, the Indian government banned all expeditions from the Sikkim side. The northeastern route has been used only three times in history.

In May 2025, an Indian Army expedition climbed Kangchenjunga from the Nepal side as part of a 'Har Shikhar Tiranga' mission. The Sikkim Indigenous Lepcha Tribal Association held purification ceremonies across the state. The Chief Minister wrote to India's Home Minister calling it a violation of both law and deeply held belief.

The mountain stands there still — climbed from Nepal, forbidden from India. Not because it's too hard. Because some things are not meant to be touched.

Should a government ban climbing to protect a mountain's soul?

text.line_1: An Indian Army team climbed from Nepal.
Some peaks are not meant to be touched.
text.line_2: Should a mountain have a soul?
